All factual claims verified against primary sources. (1) The design doc's Cargo.toml at line 91 of gistfile1.txt declares serde_yaml = "0.9". (2) GitHub API confirms dtolnay/serde-yaml is archived (archived: true, last push 2024-03-25). (3) crates.io API confirms the final release is 0.9.34+deprecated (2024-03-25), matching the finding exactly. (4) The dead-dependency claim is correct: serde_yaml appears only in the Cargo.toml; the skeleton's src/config.rs generates outrunner.yml via format!/raw string literals + std::fs::write and never imports serde_yaml. (5) The recommended alternatives are real and maintained: serde_yaml_ng (0.10.0, ~5.7M downloads) and serde_norway (0.9.42, updated Dec 2024, ~7.6M downloads) both exist on crates.io as API-compatible forks; serde_json is first-party maintained. Severity is not inflated for a design-stage review: the dependency sits on the tool's core responsibility (YAML config generation/validation), and choosing the serialization path before v1 code lands is exactly when the decision is cheapest. The only nuance is that 0.9.34+deprecated still compiles and works today — the risk is unmaintained-dependency/supply-chain, not immediate breakage — but the finding claims only that it "receives no fixes," which is accurate. The recommendation is sound and actionable.

Adversarial verification FAILED to refute the finding — every material factual claim checks out. (1) gha-outrunner (NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner, MIT) verified: ephemeral runners via GitHub's scaleset API with Docker/libvirt/Tart backends, no Kubernetes — it already does everything hard in the design. The design doc itself describes gha-isolated as 'a wrapper CLI around gha-outrunner,' and its skeleton confirms the thin-layer characterization: runner.rs is a placeholder that shells out to gha-outrunner; the only real logic is platform detection, backend selection, YAML config generation, and (unimplemented) service install. (2) GARM (cloudbase/garm) verified: active (v0.2.1 May 2026), auto-scaling ephemeral runners with pluggable providers (LXD, Incus, AWS, Azure, GCP, k8s). (3) actions/scaleset verified: GitHub's official standalone Go Runner Scale Set Client, public preview, explicitly for custom autoscalers on VMs/containers/bare metal without Kubernetes — only error is the date: announced Feb 3, 2026 (community discussion #186265), not Feb 5. (4) Cirrus Labs → OpenAI acquisition verified (announced April 7, 2026); Cirrus CI shuts down June 1, 2026, Cirrus Runners closed to new customers — the finding's 'runner products shutting down' is accurate (nuance: Tart the tool is being relicensed more permissively, not killed, but the finding didn't claim otherwise). (5) Commercial alternatives (RunsOn, actuated, Ubicloud, WarpBuild, Namespace, Blacksmith) and ARC-as-k8s-reference are all real and correctly characterized. The recommendation (evaluate GARM/gha-outrunner-as-is; scope gha-isolated as a thin installer or upstream auto-detection to MIT-licensed gha-outrunner) is sound and proportionate. Severity 'major' is justified for a build-vs-reuse design question. Only correction needed: the scaleset public-preview announcement date.
result.corrected_claim: The finding stands as written with one trivial date correction: GitHub's standalone Runner Scale Set Client (actions/scaleset) public preview was announced February 3, 2026 (community discussion #186265), not February 5, 2026. All other facts verified: gha-outrunner already provides ephemeral scaleset runners with Docker/libvirt/Tart isolation and no k8s (MIT); GARM is a mature pluggable-provider autoscaling manager; RunsOn/actuated/Ubicloud/WarpBuild cover the commercial space; ARC remains the k8s reference; Cirrus Labs' runner products are winding down after the OpenAI acquisition (Cirrus CI ends June 1, 2026; Tart itself continues under a more permissive license). gha-isolated's unique value is indeed only the thin cross-platform backend auto-detection + service-install UX on top of gha-outrunner.

result.reasoning: The finding is factually correct and could not be refuted. (1) Google's official nested-virtualization docs confirm Compute Engine exposes Intel VT-x to L1 VMs so they can run Linux KVM, enabled per-VM, with exclusions exactly as the finding states (E2, memory-optimized, AMD, Arm, H4D) — so on Intel N1/N2/C2/C3 machine types /dev/kvm exists and the design's libvirt backend works, making the doc's blanket 'Linux / GCE (no strong KVM) → Docker + Sysbox' row (gistfile1.txt lines 16, 51-55) factually wrong as a categorical claim. (2) The internal-contradiction claim is verified: the doc's own detection code (lines 179, 199-201) checks Path::new("/dev/kvm").exists() and returns Backend::Libvirt on any Linux with KVM, so on a nested-virt-enabled GCE VM the code contradicts the doc's table. (3) The finding's caveats are also accurate — Google's docs themselves state a 10%+ performance decrease for nested VMs, and AMD/Arm GCE genuinely lack nested virt. (4) Severity 'major' is proportionate: the backend-selection matrix is the central design decision and GCE is one of three named target platforms. The recommendation is sensible and fits the doc's existing 'Optional GCE helpers' module. Only minor nuance: preferring Docker+Sysbox on GCE could be a defensible deliberate performance tradeoff, but the doc frames it as a capability limitation ('no strong KVM'), which is false as stated.

I attempted to refute the finding and failed on every factual axis; independent evidence actually strengthens it.

Verification against the doc (/tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt):
(1) The words "token", "PAT", "auth", "credential", "registration", and "URL" (in the GitHub-target sense) appear nowhere in the 292-line doc. (2) The generated outrunner.yml in src/config.rs (doc lines 212-251) contains exactly backend/labels/count/resources(/docker limits) — no github section, no url, no token reference; the line citation in the finding is accurate. (3) "Use GitHub Scale Sets + ephemeral runners by default" is indeed Core Principle 3 (doc line 10). (4) The module table (section 4, lines 62-78) has no module owning credentials or registration. (5) `init` (main.rs lines 131-137) asks no repo/org/URL/token question, and runner::start is a placeholder that would invoke `gha-outrunner --config outrunner.yml` with no --url/--token flags.

Strongest refutation candidate — "auth is implicitly delegated to gha-outrunner" — fails on external evidence. gha-outrunner is a real tool (github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner), and its configuration reference (docs/reference/configuration.md) states: "Every runner must have a URL reachable via the resolution chain: per-runner url, --url flag, or this global field," plus a token resolution chain (per-runner token_file → --token → GITHUB_TOKEN env → systemd-creds → global token_file). The wrapper's whole job includes generating outrunner.yml, and the config it generates omits the mandatory url and any token_file, and the design never instructs the user to set GITHUB_TOKEN or pass flags. So the finding's sharpest claim — that `gha-isolated init && gha-isolated start` as designed cannot produce a runner that appears in GitHub — is confirmed by the upstream tool's own docs, not merely plausible. The severity (critical) is proportionate: for a turnkey runner-provisioning CLI whose init/config-generation is a first-class module, omitting the only fields that connect it to GitHub is a core-functionality gap, not a nitpick, and the doc itself claims "This skeleton is runnable today."

One minor caveat on the recommendation (not enough to refute): gha-outrunner currently supports PAT-file/env/token-flag auth only — there is no native GitHub App option per its configuration reference. "GitHub App as primary" is still implementable (the wrapper can mint installation tokens and write them to token_file, refreshing on expiry), but the recommendation should be framed as wrapper-side token minting against gha-outrunner's token_file mechanism rather than a config option that exists upstream. Similarly, since gha-outrunner uses the scaleset API for registration, the "JIT runner config" suggestion is one option among several rather than the obvious fit. The rest of the recommendation (scoping as an init question, token lifecycle, 0600 credential storage outside outrunner.yml/env) aligns directly with gha-outrunner's actual token_file design and is sound.

Sources: [NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner](https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner), [gha-outrunner configuration reference](https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner/blob/main/docs/reference/configuration.md)
result.corrected_claim: The finding stands as written, with one refinement to the recommendation: gha-outrunner natively supports only PAT-style tokens (token_file / --token / GITHUB_TOKEN / systemd-creds) and a mandatory runner URL — it has no built-in GitHub App auth. The design should therefore specify (a) collecting the target URL and scope at `gha-isolated init`, (b) writing url + token_file into the generated outrunner.yml (or documenting the env/flag path), and (c) if GitHub App auth is desired as primary, wrapper-side installation-token minting/refresh into the token_file path rather than assuming an upstream github: config section.

result.reasoning: All factual assertions verified against the design doc (/tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt). (1) Line 203 is exactly `return Backend::DockerSysbox; // assume Sysbox is installed or will be`. (2) platform.rs only checks /dev/kvm and `which tart`; nothing verifies docker daemon, sysbox-runc, or Docker runtimes — config.rs's DockerSysbox arm doesn't even emit sysbox config. (3) The selection table (doc lines 56–60) does document a 'Fallback → docker' row. (4) Tracing select(): on Linux, has_kvm → Libvirt, else unconditionally DockerSysbox, so Backend::Docker is genuinely unreachable on Linux — the documented fallback is dead code on its target platform. (5) The GCE failure scenario is realistic: GCE VMs lack /dev/kvm unless nested virt is explicitly enabled, so a fresh VM without sysbox selects DockerSysbox and `docker run --runtime=sysbox-runc` would fail with 'unknown runtime'. The doc claims the skeleton is 'runnable today' and its Data Flow step 4 promises prerequisite checks the design never specifies, so this is a design gap, not merely an unimplemented stub. The recommendation (capability-driven selection, bail on unknown OS) is standard and sound; 'major' severity is proportionate since the function's sole job is backend selection and it contradicts the design's own table. One minor inaccuracy: the Docker arm is reachable not only on unknown OSes but also on macOS without Tart (line 196 requires os=='macos' && has_tart) — this understates the problem and strengthens, not weakens, the finding.
result.corrected_claim: The finding stands as written, with one correction: the `Backend::Docker` arm (line 205) is reachable not only when os is neither macos nor linux, but also on macOS when Tart is not installed — so the silent, capability-unchecked Docker default covers unknown OSes AND Tart-less Macs, while remaining unreachable on Linux where the table documents it as the fallback.

result.reasoning: Verified directly against the design doc: sysinfo = "0.30" is in the Cargo.toml skeleton (line 93); platform.rs's detect() creates `let mut sys = System::new_all(); sys.refresh_all();` (lines 166-167) and never reads sys — all actual detection uses cfg!/env::consts/Path::exists/which. sysinfo appears nowhere else in the skeleton, so the dep is dead weight. config.rs hardcodes memory: 4G, cpus: 2, count: 2, and doc line 11 states the goal 'Apply consistent resource protection everywhere', validating recommendation (b). The only factual error is the claim that 'the compiler will even warn about the unused variable' — it will not, because sys.refresh_all() is a use of the variable and mut is required by refresh_all(&mut self); the code compiles warning-free. That detail error slightly strengthens the finding (the dead work is silent) and does not change the verdict. result.corrected_claim: The finding is correct except one detail: rustc will NOT emit an unused-variable warning, because `sys.refresh_all()` is a use of `sys` (and `mut` is required by `refresh_all(&mut self)`). result.reasoning: Every factual claim in the finding checks out against the repo and git history. (1) Fleet is two hosts: self-hosted-oss README/launchd files = Mac Colima stack; self-hosted-colima README/install.sh = Jeff-Ubuntu with systemd --user colima-runners.service + Lima VM + 16-runner compose (org-runner-16 present in docker-compose.yml; README's '10' is stale). No GCE setup exists in either stack. (2) Duplicate pre-job-hook fix confirmed: oss disk-cleanup added in 8c34a4f1bf (PR #7770), colima copy in 0794779451 ('mirrors self-hosted-oss/ version'); diff shows near-identical logic with drift, and b2c58658cd was a third fix needed for the colima copy — real drift cost. (3) The Rust skeleton in the design doc has ~30-45 lines of real logic (platform detect, backend select, hardcoded YAML template); runner.rs is all placeholders and service install 'not yet implemented' — 100% of operationally hard code deferred, and the design merely wraps NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner, a real existing tool that already implements Tart/libvirt/Docker per-job ephemeral runners, so no custom Rust binary is needed even for the pilot (a static outrunner.yml per host + bash install script suffices at 2 hosts). (4) The recommendation matches repo policy (CLAUDE.md 'Self-Hosted Runner Changes — must be in git and portable', install.sh as source of truth) and the existing monitor/alert/cleanup mesh (monitor.sh, slack-alert.sh, heal-runners.sh, cleanup scripts). Severity 'major' is proportionate since the finding rejects the doc's core deliverable with sound factual basis. Only cosmetic quibbles found (stale README runner count, 'rewrite' vs 'wrapper' wording), neither of which undermines the finding.
